title: Online-Optimized RAG for Tool Use and Function Calling
author: Pan, Yu, Li, Xiaocheng, Wang, Hanzhao
summary: This paper addresses embedding misalignment in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems used for tool selection and function calling. The authors propose Online-Optimized RAG, a deployment-time framework that continuously adapts retrieval embeddings using minimal feedback (e.g., task success signals) from live interactions. The method applies lightweight online gradient updates with negligible per-query latency and requires no modifications to the underlying LLM. The framework supports single/multi-hop tool use, dynamic tool inventories, and K-retrieval with re-ranking. Technical readers should care because this addresses a critical practical problem: imperfect embedding models often fail to correctly match queries to tool descriptions, causing task failures. The paper provides theoretical analysis and demonstrates consistent improvements in tool selection accuracy and end-task success across diverse scenarios, offering a plug-and-play path toward robust, self-improving RAG systems.
content: Computer Science > Software Engineering arXiv:2509.20415 (cs) [Submitted on 24 Sep 2025 ( v1 ), last revised 26 Sep 2025 (this version, v2)] Title: Online-Optimized RAG for Tool Use and Function Calling Authors: Yu Pan , Xiaocheng Li , Hanzhao Wang View a PDF of the paper titled Online-Optimized RAG for Tool Use and Function Calling, by Yu Pan and 2 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: In many applications, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) drives tool use and function calling by embedding the (user) queries and matching them to pre-specified tool/function descriptions. In this paper, we address an embedding misalignment issue that often arises in practical applications due to imperfect embedding models or noisy descriptions; such misalignment may lead to incorrect retrieval and task failure. We introduce Online-Optimized RAG, a deployment-time framework that continually adapts retrieval embeddings from live interactions using minimal feedback (e.g., task success). Online-Optimized RAG applies lightweight online gradient updates with negligible per-query latency and requires no changes to the underlying LLM. The method is plug-and-play: it supports both single- and multi-hop tool use, dynamic tool inventories, and $K$-retrieval with re-ranking. We provide a problem-dependent theoretical analysis that quantifies how the method's performance depends on the initialization quality of the embeddings and other related quantities. Across diverse tool-use and document-retrieval scenarios, our Online-Optimized RAG consistently improves tool selection accuracy and end-task success, thus providing a simple, practical path to robust, self-improving RAG systems. takeaways: Online gradient updates adapt embeddings at deployment time using only task success signals, eliminating need for labeled data or model retraining.
Negligible per-query latency and plug-and-play design enable integration with existing RAG systems without modifying the LLM.
Theoretical analysis quantifies performance dependence on embedding initialization quality and other factors, providing principled optimization guidance.
Method supports multi-hop tool use, dynamic tool inventories, and K-retrieval with re-ranking for practical production scenarios.

title: IntellAgent: A Multi-Agent Framework for Evaluating Conversational AI Systems
author: Levi, Elad, Kadar, Ilan
summary: IntellAgent is an open-source multi-agent framework for systematically evaluating conversational AI systems at scale. Traditional evaluation methods struggle with the complexity of multi-turn dialogues, API integration, and policy constraints in deployed agents. The framework automates synthetic benchmark creation using policy-driven graph modeling, realistic event generation, and interactive simulations to provide fine-grained diagnostics beyond coarse-grained metrics. By modeling policy interactions as graphs and simulating multi-policy scenarios, IntellAgent identifies performance gaps and offers actionable insights for optimization. This addresses a critical gap in bridging research and production deployment of task-oriented LLM agents.
content: Computer Science > Computation and Language arXiv:2501.11067 (cs) [Submitted on 19 Jan 2025] Title: IntellAgent: A Multi-Agent Framework for Evaluating Conversational AI Systems Authors: Elad Levi , Ilan Kadar View a PDF of the paper titled IntellAgent: A Multi-Agent Framework for Evaluating Conversational AI Systems, by Elad Levi and 1 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are transforming artificial intelligence, evolving into task-oriented systems capable of autonomous planning and execution. One of the primary applications of LLMs is conversational AI systems, which must navigate multi-turn dialogues, integrate domain-specific APIs, and adhere to strict policy constraints. However, evaluating these agents remains a significant challenge, as traditional methods fail to capture the complexity and variability of real-world interactions. We introduce IntellAgent, a scalable, open-source multi-agent framework designed to evaluate conversational AI systems comprehensively. IntellAgent automates the creation of diverse, synthetic benchmarks by combining policy-driven graph modeling, realistic event generation, and interactive user-agent simulations. This innovative approach provides fine-grained diagnostics, addressing the limitations of static and manually curated benchmarks with coarse-grained metrics. IntellAgent represents a paradigm shift in evaluating conversational AI. By simulating realistic, multi-policy scenarios across varying levels of complexity, IntellAgent captures the nuanced interplay of agent capabilities and policy constraints. Unlike traditional methods, it employs a graph-based policy model to represent relationships, likelihoods, and complexities of policy interactions, enabling highly detailed diagnostics. IntellAgent also identifies critical performance gaps, offering actionable insights for targeted optimization. Its modular, open-source design supports seamless integration of new domains, policies, and APIs, fostering reproducibility and community collaboration. Our findings demonstrate that IntellAgent serves as an effective framework for advancing conversational AI by addressing challenges in bridging research and deployment. takeaways: IntellAgent uses graph-based policy models to represent policy interactions and complexities in conversational AI evaluation.
Synthetic benchmarks with automated generation capture real-world multi-turn dialogue variability better than static test sets.
Framework provides fine-grained diagnostics identifying critical performance gaps for targeted agent optimization.
Modular, open-source design enables domain, policy, and API integration for reproducibility and collaboration.
